SUMMARY:
High-Entropy Alloys are advanced materials composed of more than four principal elements, known for their unique properties such as exceptional mechanical strength, corrosion resistance, and thermal stability. High-entropy alloys (HEAs), composed of multiple components at equal or near molar concentrations and located in the unknown center area of the phase diagram [1], have been progressively attracting significant interesting, due to many exceptional properties, such as high strength and hardness, extraordinary ductility and fracture toughness, corrosion resistance and so on [2,3]. In 14 years, high entropy alloys (HEAs) have been among the most studied materials in the metallurgical field. High-entropy alloys (HEAs) are presently of great research interest in materials science and engineering.

[2022, Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/journal-of-magnetism-and-magnetic-materials)Priyanka Kumari, ... Rohit R. Shahi

### Abstract

Alloys-based materials have now become advanced from conventional alloys to multi-principal elements and open new areas of applications. High Entropy Alloys are drawn considerable attention because of their unique properties, which can be tuned based on the requirements. High entropy materials have extraordinary mechanical properties, [corrosion resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/corrosion-resistance "Learn more about corrosion resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), thermal stability, and other promising functional properties, which makes them to be used as a catalysis for water splitting, electrodes in batteries for charge storage in supercapacitor and as a hydrogen storage material. Thus the high entropy alloy now becomes an emerging advanced material. Moreover, in the last six years, several research articles appeared which described the excellent [magnetic properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/magnetic-property "Learn more about magnetic properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of [high entropy alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about high entropy alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). More than four principal elements help to tune the magnetic properties along with other properties like mechanical, corrosion, etc., which are required for industrial applications of magnetic materials. The magnetic properties of [HEAs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about HEAs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are highly dependent on the phase structure, microstructure, synthesis route, processing parameter, and alloying elements. This review article summarized the different features of high entropy alloys, core effects, synthesis methods, and state-of-the-art magnetic properties of the High Entropy alloys. A separate section is also devoted to the magnetic properties of high entropy [oxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxide-compound "Learn more about oxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Also, this review is underlying different principles that have importance on the future development of magnetic HEAsfor industrial applications.

### 1 Introduction

[High entropy alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about High entropy alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HEAs) are a class of sophisticated metallic materials that typically contain five or more primary elements in near-equal atomic concentrations (usually ranging from 5 to 35 at. %) [1–5]. Their high configurational entropy arises from the many possible ways to distribute each element on a lattice. The unique composition of [HEAs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about HEAs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") results in a complex microstructure, leading to remarkable mechanical properties such as high [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ductility, toughness, and [corrosion resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/corrosion-resistance "Learn more about corrosion resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Furthermore, these alloys also exhibit exceptional thermal stability and wear resistance, making them suitable for many applications in aerospace, automotive, energy, and other industries [6–8].

High-entropy alloys (HEAs) have gained significant attention due to their unique characteristics, particularly the substantial configurational entropy arising from the distribution of elements on a lattice. This article delves into the concept of high configurational entropy resulting from the random arrangement of multiple elements within crystallographic sites.

An alternate perspective focuses on the mixing entropy (Δ S m i x), expressed as Δ S m i x=−R∑i=1 n c i ln c i, where R, n and c i denote the gas constant, the number of elements, and the atomic fraction, respectively [3,9]. Values of Δ S m i x less than −0.69 R characterize low entropy alloys; those exceeding −1.6 R correspond to [high entropy alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about high entropy alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); and intermediate values indicate [medium entropy alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/medium-entropy-alloy "Learn more about medium entropy alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [3,9].

Increased configurational entropy is primarily a result of incorporating a greater number of constituent elements, which contribute significantly to the stabilization of crystal structures without substantially affecting the transition temperature [3]. This phenomenon is central to the emergence of HEAs, which exhibit both high randomness in their lattice structures and exceptional physical and mechanical properties, prompting extensive research interest [3].

HEAs are distinguished by the random arrangement of atoms inside the crystallographic locations of simple lattices, resulting in alloys with high configurational entropy such as face-centered cubic (FCC), body-centered cubic (BCC), and hexagonal close-packed (hcp) [3]. The thermodynamic stability equation given by the Gibbs energy (Δ G m i x=Δ H m i x−T Δ S m i x) governs the production of HEAs [10]. The formation of solid-solution single-phase HEAs results from the interaction of various elements' entropy and enthalpy. Entropy mixing intensifies as the number of constituent elements rises, and it also becomes a driving force in the development of single-phase mixtures [10]. Entropy mixing becomes dominant when the enthalpy of interactions (Δ H i j) possesses small values. Instances where the enthalpy of interaction (Δ H i j) between pairs of elements is close to zero result in a natural formation of high entropy mixing with random atomic arrangements. However, such cases are relatively uncommon in alloy formation. Highly positive Δ H m i x values lead to segregation and immiscibility with heterogeneous structures, while strongly negative values result in [intermetallic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/intermetallics "Learn more about intermetallic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ordering [10]. In the context of HEAs, the preference is for solid solution formation over [intermetallic compounds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/intermetallics "Learn more about intermetallic compounds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The concept of ideal entropy of mixing is employed for functional purposes due to the challenges in defining the value of Δ H m i x.

Recent research has been conducted on the superconducting properties of HEAs. For example, in the Ta–Nb-Hf-Zr-Ti system, type-II bulk superconducting features were observed, with a critical temperature (_T_ _c_) of 7.3 K [11] and the superconducting state remaining stable under high pressure up to 190 GPa [12]. Other studies have revealed compositionally modified (TaNb)1-x(HfZrTi)x [13], Nb-Re-Hf-Zr-Ti (_T_ _c_=5.3 K) [14], (ScZrNb)1-x(RhPd)x [15], and Sc-Zr-Nb-Ta-RhPd (_T_ _c_=9.3 K) [13]. These results suggest that these HEAs work like ordinary s-wave phonon-mediated [superconductors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/superconducting-material "Learn more about superconductors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Furthermore, the superconducting state resists disorder, as the transition temperature in the simple crystalline and [amorphous](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/amorphous-material "Learn more about amorphous from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") metallic states is unaffected.

Nb 3 Ge has the highest critical temperature (_T_ _c_) of 23.2 K and the highest critical field (_H_ _c_) of 37 T of any intermetallic [superconductor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/superconducting-material "Learn more about superconductor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). A chemical transport reaction forms this [thin film](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/thin-films "Learn more about thin film from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on a sapphire substrate [16]. Nb3Sn, a commercially accessible superconductor with a _T_ _c_ of 18.3 K and _H_ _c_ of 30 T, is utilized in high-field magnet applications such as thermonuclear fusion reactors, [particle accelerator](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/particle-acceleration "Learn more about particle accelerator from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") magnets, [magnetic resonance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/magnetic-resonance "Learn more about magnetic resonance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") imaging (MRI), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) [17,18]. HEAs currently have lower critical temperatures and critical current densities than the Nb 3 Sn superconductor, which has a high critical current density (_J_ _c_) of 3000 A/mm 2.

Despite current superconducting property limitations, HEAs have enormous potential for practical superconducting applications due to their outstanding [mechanical strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about mechanical strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), resistance to fracture, wear, corrosion, and [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and high thermal and [structural stability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/structural-stability "Learn more about structural stability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). HEAs could become more relevant in this stage by improving their superconducting properties through material design and process engineering. However, because this field of study is still in its infancy, there needs to be more exploration into the fundamental knowledge of the physical properties of [HEA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about HEA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") superconductors.

Recently, it was claimed that increasing the VEC in both crystalline and amorphous [transition metal alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/transition-metal-alloys "Learn more about transition metal alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") could increase the critical temperatures of HEAs superconductors [19]. However, this approach is still controversial because it has yet to be demonstrated that the critical temperature depends on the VEC [20]. The study of the superconducting characteristics of HEAs is primarily concerned with determining how these qualities alter with variations in elemental composition.

Based on VEC studies in both the normal and superconducting states, it is reported the unique features of the superconducting compound Ta 1/6 Nb 2/6 Hf 1/6 Zr 1/6 Ti 1/6, which is essentially optimally doped. In the normal state, the temperature-dependent electrical resistivity (_ρ(T)_) demonstrated a parabolic dependency around the critical temperature (_T_ _c_), changing to linear behavior at higher temperatures. Despite being close to the dirty limit, the compound had a reasonably substantial [specific heat](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/specific-heat "Learn more about specific heat from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") jump (_ΔC/γT_ _c_). It is also discovered that the compound's Kadowaki-Woods ratios (_A/γ_ _2_) closely approach those of heavy-fermion states, indicating a strongly correlated system with a significant effective mass (29 _m_ _e_). The presence of strongly correlated electron behavior in s-wave [superconductivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/superconductivity "Learn more about superconductivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is crucial since many superconductors in such systems are heavy-fermion and high-_T_ _c_[cuprate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/cuprates "Learn more about cuprate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") superconductors, both of which are nodal superconductors. This research adds to our expanding understanding of the HEA Ta 1/6 Nb 2/6 Hf 1/6 Zr 1/6 Ti 1/6 compound's unique superconducting and physical properties and its future applications as a metallic superconductor.

Because of the attractive mix of mechanical and electrical properties, the early stages of HEA research concentrated mostly on elements from the 4 _d_ and 5 _d_ series. Based on each atom's crystal structure and VEC, superconductivity in HEAs can be categorized into four categories. The VEC is strongly related to the superconducting transition temperature (_T_ _c_) of HEA superconductors [3]. The VEC range of Type-A HEA superconductors is 4.3–4.8 [21], which has an early transition metal composition [3]. They feature a BCC structure. Type-B HEA superconductors, on the other hand, have a more complicated structure with a different VEC range of 6.5–6.8 [15]. Type-B involves 5 _d_ transition metals, as demonstrated by (HfTaWIr)(1-x)Re x (x<0.6) [3]. The CsCl-type structure of Type-C HEA superconductors has a VEC range of 5.9–6.3, whereas Type-C mixes early and late transition metals with a CsCl-type lattice, providing the greatest _T_ _c_ among HEA superconductors, as demonstrated by ScZrNbTa)0.65(RhPd)0.35 [3]. Type-D HEA superconductors with a hexagonal-close-packed (HCP) structure and a VEC value 5.8 have recently been reported [14] as demonstrated by compositions such as Re 0.56 Nb 0.11 Ti 0.11 Zr 0.11 Hf 0.1 [3].

The HEA composed of Ta 34 Nb 33 Hf 8 Zr 14 Ti 11 known as the first HEAs which exhibits superconducting properties, possesses an energy gap in the electronic [density of states](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/density-of-states "Learn more about density of states from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (DOS) at the Fermi level of approximately _2Δ_≈2.2 meV. This particular HEA aligns closely with a Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer (BCS)-type phonon-mediated superconductor within the confines of a weak electron-phonon coupling limit, thereby classifying it as a “dirty” superconductor. At lower temperatures, where entropic stabilization is ineffective, the formation of a superconducting gap contributes to the electronic stabilization of the HEA state. However, the electronic energy gain resulting from the superconducting transition is insufficient for the global stabilization of the disordered state, rendering it metastable. This highlights the complex interplay between the electronic and lattice degrees of freedom in these materials, and the delicate balance required to achieve superconductivity in such a diverse alloy system [11]. K. Jasiewicz et al. conducted calculations with a focus on estimating the electron-phonon coupling constant λ of the same material HEA Ta 34 Nb 33 Hf 8 Zr 14 Ti 11. They discovered that the estimated λ=1.16 closely aligns with the value determined from specific heat measurements, λ=0.98. This suggests a rather strong electron-phonon coupling in this material [22]. Recently, a study conducted on the 600-nm thin film of superconducting HEAsNb 67 Hf 11 Ti 11 Zr 11 and Nb 35 Ta 35 Hf 10 Ti 10 Zr 10 utilized point-contact spectroscopy measurements [23]. The research, led by Gabriel Pristáš et al., was able to directly observe the temperature development of the superconducting energy gap Δ _(T)_ and determine the superconducting coupling strength _2_ Δ _/k_ _B_ _T_ _c_=3.54 and _2_ Δ _/k_ _B_ _T_ _c_=4.21, respectively. These findings align with those of conventional s-wave phonon-mediated Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer superconductors.

The Ta 1/6 Nb 2/6 Hf 1/6 Zr 1/6 Ti 1/6 HEA superconductor, synthesized by the arc-melting technique, demonstrated strongly correlated and strongly coupled s-wave superconducting properties. The strong correlation and coupling were evident from various superconducting parameters, including a high Kadowaki-Woods ratio, a substantial specific heat jump, and a high effective mass. However, the superconducting properties of the Ta 1/6 Nb 2/6 Hf 1/6 Zr 1/6 Ti 1/6 compound produced through planetary ball milling and SPS method differed superconducting properties significantly. The compound has a valence electron count (VEC) of 4.5. While the exceptional mechanical strength of HEA compounds is essential for practical applications, it also poses difficulties in shaping them through cutting and grinding due to their extreme strength. Powder [metallurgical processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/metallurgical-process "Learn more about metallurgical processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") like ball milling and SPS can expand the scope of HEA applications by facilitating the production of samples with different shapes, including those suitable for [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/3d-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") purposes.

When comparing the Ta 1/6 Nb 2/6 Hf 1/6 Zr 1/6 Ti 1/6 HEA samples processed through SPS and arc-melting, it is observed a significant improvement in critical current density and pinning force in the ball-milled and SPS-sintered sample. The SPS-sintered sample exhibited a strong vortex pinning force, leading to stable [remanent magnetization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/remanent-magnetization "Learn more about remanent magnetization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") after removing the applied [magnetic field](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/magnetic-field "Learn more about magnetic field from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Additionally, it is observed [magnetic flux](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/magnetic-flux "Learn more about magnetic flux from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") avalanches during magnetic hysteresis loop measurements at low temperatures (_T_≤4 K) and low magnetic field regions (_H_≤1 T). Vortex avalanches can be explained by either vortex quantum creep or flux jump at weakly coupled superconducting grain boundaries.

### 4.2 High-entropy alloys

High-entropy alloys (HEAs) are also called principal-component free alloys or multi-principal alloys, which consist of five, six, or more components in more or less equal proportions and have unique characteristics[119]. Du et al.[87] prepared FeCoNiCrAl high-entropy coatings at a scanning speed of 50 m/min. The coatings had high [hardness values](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hardness-value "Learn more about hardness values from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on different substrates (C45 and 304SS). Xu et al.[93] deposited a CuAlNiCrFe high-entropy alloy bonding layer by [EHLA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/laser-cladding "Learn more about EHLA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Compared with conventional [HEA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about HEA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") coatings prepared by high-velocity oxygen-fuel(HVOF), the bonded layer of the CuAlNiCrFe HEA showed completely different preoxidation characteristics, and the time required for the formation of an [alumina](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aluminum-oxide "Learn more about alumina from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") protective film was shorter after EHLA treatment, which can be explained by Fig. 22. The bulk structure of the high-entropy alloy bond-layer cladding formed by the high-speed laser is different from the layer structures of traditional MCrAlY coatings. Traditional MCrAlY coatings are based on thermal spraying technology, and the layered-interface block effect of the unbound interface, [surface oxides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/surface-oxide "Learn more about surface oxides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and partial metallurgical bonding leads to the slow diffusion of [aluminum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aluminum "Learn more about aluminum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the MCrAlY coating. When a coating is exposed to a high-temperature atmospheric environment, the diffused aluminum content is not sufficient to form a continuous alumina layer on the surface of the bonding layer, and the layered interface hinders the diffusion of elements. Hence, non-pre-oxidized MCrAlY coatings sometimes produce spinel structures at high temperatures[120]. The high-entropy alloy bonding layers formed by EHLA have block structures and good metallurgical bonding with the substrate. In the initial stage of [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), aluminum can easily diffuse to form an alumina protective layer, which leads to the pre-oxidation time of this new coating being very short. In addition, it can form continuous α-Al 2 O 3 thermally grown oxides (TGO) to avoid the formation of other oxides and spinel structures. Finally, with the consumption of aluminum, although the phase structure of the high-entropy alloy bonding layer changes from body-centered cubic to face-centered cubic, it still maintains a stable simple solid solution structure.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Alloys with high proportions of several metals

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Atomic_structure_model_of_fcc_CoCrFeMnNi.png/250px-Atomic_structure_model_of_fcc_CoCrFeMnNi.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Atomic_structure_model_of_fcc_CoCrFeMnNi.png)

Atomic structure model of fcc CoCrFeMnNi[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Wang1-1)

**High-entropy alloys** (**HEAs**) are [alloys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alloy "Alloy") that are formed by mixing equal or relatively large proportions of (usually) five or more [elements](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_element "Chemical element"). Prior to the synthesis of these substances, typical [metal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal "Metal") alloys comprised one or two major components with smaller amounts of other elements. For example, additional elements can be added to [iron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iron "Iron") to improve its properties, thereby creating an iron-based alloy, but typically in fairly low proportions, such as the proportions of [carbon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon "Carbon"), [manganese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manganese "Manganese"), and others in various [steels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steel "Steel").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-tsai-2) Hence, high-entropy alloys are a novel class of materials.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Wang1-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-tsai-2) The term "high-entropy alloys" was coined by [Taiwanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") scientist [Jien-Wei Yeh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jein-Wei_Yeh "Jein-Wei Yeh")[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-3) because the [entropy increase of mixing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entropy_of_mixing "Entropy of mixing") is substantially higher when there is a larger number of elements in the mix, and their proportions are more nearly equal.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-4) Some alternative names, such as multi-component alloys, compositionally complex alloys and multi-principal-element alloys are also suggested by other researchers.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:9-6) Compositionally complex alloys (CCAs) are an up-and-coming group of materials due to their unique mechanical properties. They have high strength and toughness, the ability to operate at higher temperatures than current alloys, and have superior ductility. Material ductility is important because it quantifies the permanent deformation a material can withstand before failure, a key consideration in designing safe and reliable materials. Due to their enhanced properties, CCAs show promise in extreme environments. An extreme environment presents significant challenges for a material to perform to its intended use within designated safety limits. CCAs can be used in several applications such as aerospace propulsion systems, land-based gas turbines, heat exchangers, and the chemical process industry.

These alloys are currently the focus of significant attention in [materials science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Materials_science "Materials science") and engineering because they have potentially desirable properties.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-tsai-2) Furthermore, research indicates that some HEAs have considerably better [strength-to-weight ratios](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strength-to-weight_ratio "Strength-to-weight ratio"), with a higher degree of [fracture resistance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fracture_mechanics "Fracture mechanics"), [tensile strength](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensile_strength "Tensile strength"), and [corrosion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corrosion "Corrosion") and [oxidation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidation "Oxidation") resistance than conventional alloys.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-8)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-9) Although HEAs have been studied since the 1980s, research substantially accelerated in the 2010s.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-tsai-2)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:9-6)[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-lavine-10)[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-shipman-11)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Youssef-12)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-cryogenic-13)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-gludov-14)

Development
-----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Development")]

Although HEAs were considered from a theoretical standpoint as early as 1981[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-15) and 1996,[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-16) and throughout the 1980s, in 1995 [Taiwanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") scientist [Jien-Wei Yeh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jein-Wei_Yeh "Jein-Wei Yeh") came up with his idea for ways of actually creating high-entropy alloys, while driving through the [Hsinchu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hsinchu "Hsinchu"), Taiwan, countryside. Soon after, he decided to begin creating these special alloys in his lab, being in the only region researching these alloys for over a decade. Most countries in [Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Europe "Europe"), the [United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States "United States"), and other parts of the world lagged behind in the development of HEAs. Significant research interest from other countries did not develop until after 2004 when Yeh and his team of scientists built the world's first high-entropy alloys to withstand extremely high temperatures and pressures.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-17) Potential applications include use in state-of-the-art race cars, spacecraft, submarines, nuclear reactors,[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Sonal1-18) jet aircraft, nuclear weapons, long range [hypersonic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypersonic "Hypersonic")[missiles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hsiung_Feng_III "Hsiung Feng III"), and so on.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-19)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-20)

A few months later, after the publication of Yeh's paper, another independent paper on high-entropy alloys was published by a team from the [United Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") composed of [Brian Cantor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brian_Cantor "Brian Cantor"), I. T. H. Chang, P. Knight, and A. J. B. Vincent. Yeh was also the first to coin the term "high-entropy alloy" when he attributed the high configurational entropy as the mechanism stabilizing the [solid solution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solid_solution "Solid solution") phase.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Yeh-AEM_2004-21) Cantor did the first work in the field in the late 1970s and early 1980s, though he did not publish until 2004. Unaware of Yeh's work, he did not describe his new materials as "high-entropy" alloys, preferring the term "multicomponent alloys". The base alloy he developed, equiatomic CrMnFeCoNi, has been the subject of considerable work in the field, and is known as the "Cantor alloy", with similar derivatives known as Cantor alloys.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Cantor_2004-22) It was one of the first HEAs to be reported to form a single-phase FCC ([face-centred cubic crystal structure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cubic_crystal_system "Cubic crystal system")) solid solution.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-23)

Before the classification of high-entropy alloys and multi-component systems as a separate class of materials, nuclear scientists had already studied a system that can now be classified as a high-entropy alloy: within [nuclear fuels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuclear_fuel "Nuclear fuel") Mo-Pd-Rh-Ru-Tc particles form at grain boundaries and at fission gas bubbles.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Middleburgh_2015-24) Understanding the behavior of these "five-metal particles" was of specific interest to the medical industry because [Tc-99m](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Technetium-99m "Technetium-99m") is an important [medical imaging](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medical_imaging "Medical imaging") isotope.

Definition
----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Definition")]

There is no universally agreed-upon definition of a HEA. The originally defined HEAs as alloys containing at least 5 elements with concentrations between 5 and 35 [atomic percent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atomic_percent "Atomic percent").[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Yeh-AEM_2004-21) Later research however, suggested that this definition could be expanded. Otto et al. suggested that only alloys that form a solid solution with no [intermetallic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intermetallic "Intermetallic") phases should be considered true high-entropy alloys, because the formation of ordered phases decreases the entropy of the system.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-enthalpy_effect-25) Some authors have described four-component alloys as high-entropy alloys[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Size-dependent_plasticity_in_an_Nb2-26) while others have suggested that alloys meeting the other requirements of HEAs, but with only 2–4 elements[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-zenodo.org-27) or a mixing entropy between [_R_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ideal_gas_constant "Ideal gas constant") and 1.5 _R_[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-28) should be considered "medium-entropy" alloys.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-zenodo.org-27)

The four core effects of HEAs
-----------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The four core effects of HEAs")]

Due to their multi-component composition, HEAs exhibit different basic effects than other traditional alloys that are based only on one or two elements. Those different effects are called "the four core effects of HEAs" and are behind a lot of the particular microstructure and properties of HEAs.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-29) The four core effects are high entropy, severe lattice distortion, sluggish diffusion, and cocktail effects.

### High entropy effect

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: High entropy effect")]

The high entropy effect is the most important effect because it can enhance the formation of solid solutions and makes the microstructure much simpler than expected. Prior knowledge expected multi component alloys to have many different interactions among elements and thus form many different kinds of binary, ternary, and quaternary compounds and/or segregated phases. Thus, such alloys would possess complicated structures, brittle by nature. This expectation in fact neglects the effect of high entropy. Indeed, according to the [second law of thermodynamics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_law_of_thermodynamics "Second law of thermodynamics"), the state having the lowest mixing [Gibbs free energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gibbs_free_energy "Gibbs free energy")Δ G m i x=Δ H m i x−T Δ S m i x{\displaystyle \Delta G_{mix}=\Delta H_{mix}-T\Delta S_{mix}}![Image 5: {\displaystyle \Delta G_{mix}=\Delta H_{mix}-T\Delta S_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b38c4738d6a6fd6ee33583758e127a700154eb01) among all possible states would be the equilibrium state. Elemental phases based on one major element have small[enthalpy of mixing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enthalpy_of_mixing "Enthalpy of mixing") (Δ H m i x{\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}![Image 6: {\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/82ce3b8b3345b674fd7bfae037d37db7afa79ca0)) and a small [entropy of mixing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entropy_of_mixing "Entropy of mixing") (Δ S m i x{\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}![Image 7: {\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/deec63413959f6306dcb4fa1810f907e111cfe2d)), and compound phases have large Δ H m i x{\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}![Image 8: {\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/82ce3b8b3345b674fd7bfae037d37db7afa79ca0)but small Δ S m i x{\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}![Image 9: {\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/deec63413959f6306dcb4fa1810f907e111cfe2d); on the other hand, solid-solution phases containing multiple elements have medium Δ H m i x{\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}![Image 10: {\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/82ce3b8b3345b674fd7bfae037d37db7afa79ca0) and high Δ S m i x{\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}![Image 11: {\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/deec63413959f6306dcb4fa1810f907e111cfe2d). As a result, solid-solution phases become highly competitive for equilibrium state and more stable especially at high temperatures.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:22-30)

### Severe lattice distortion effect

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Severe lattice distortion effect")]

[![Image 12](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Lattice_distortion_Effect.svg/365px-Lattice_distortion_Effect.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Lattice_distortion_Effect.svg)

Schematic diagram showing large lattice distortion existing in the five component BCC lattice

Because [solid solution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solid_solution "Solid solution") phases with multi-principal elements are usually found in HEAs, the conventional crystal structure concept is thus extended from a one or two element basis to a multi-element basis. Every atom is surrounded by different kinds of atoms and thus suffers lattice strain and stress mainly due to atomic size difference. Besides the atomic size difference, both different bonding energy and crystal structure tendency among constituent elements are also believed to cause even higher lattice distortion because non-symmetrical bindings and electronic structure exist between an atom and its first neighbours. This distortion is believed to be the source of some of the mechanical, thermal, electrical, optical, and chemical behaviour of HEAs. Thus, overall lattice distortion would be more severe than that in traditional alloys in which most matrix atoms (or solvent atoms) have the same kind of atoms as their surroundings.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:22-30)

### Sluggish diffusion effect

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sluggish diffusion effect")]

As explained in the last section, an HEA mainly contains a random solid solution and/or an ordered solid solution. Their matrices could be regarded as whole-solute matrices. In HEAs, those whole-solute matrices' diffusion vacancies are surrounded by different element atoms, and thus have a specific lattice potential energy (LPE). This large fluctuation of LPE between lattice sites leads to low-LPE sites, serving as traps and hindering atomic diffusion.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-31) This leads to the sluggish diffusion effect.

### Cocktail effect

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cocktail effect")]

The cocktail effect is used to emphasise the enhancement of the alloy's properties by at least five major elements. Because HEAs might have one or more phases, the whole properties are from the overall contribution of the constituent phases. Besides, each phase is a solid solution and can be viewed as a composite with properties coming not only from the basic properties of the constituent, but by the mixture rule also from the interactions among all the constituents and from severe lattice distortion. The cocktail effect takes into account the effect from the atomic-scale multicomponent phases and from the multiple composite phases at the micro scale.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-32)

Alloy design
------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Alloy design")]

In conventional alloy design, one primary element such as iron, copper, or aluminum is chosen for its properties. Then, small amounts of additional elements are added to improve or add properties. Even among binary alloy systems, there are few common cases of both elements being used in nearly-equal proportions such as [Pb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lead "Lead")-[Sn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tin "Tin")[solders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solder "Solder"). Therefore, much is known from experimental results about phases near the edges of [binary phase diagrams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_phase_diagram "Binary phase diagram") and the corners of [ternary phase diagrams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ternary_plot "Ternary plot") and much less is known about phases near the centers. In higher-order (4+ components) systems that cannot be easily represented on a two-dimensional phase diagram, virtually nothing is known.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Cantor_2004-22)

Early research of HEA was focussed on forming single-phased solid solution, which could maximize the major features of high entropy alloy: high entropy, sluggish diffusion, severe lattice distortion, and cocktail effects. It has been pointed out that most successful materials need some secondary phase to strengthen the material,[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-33)[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-34) and that any HEA used in application will have a multiphase microstructure.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:21-35) However, it is still important to form single-phased material since a single-phased sample is essential for understanding the underlying mechanism of HEAs and testing specific microstructures to find structures producing special properties.[[35]](https://en.wi

### 6 High Entropy Alloys

High entropy alloys are a group of unique metals based on mixing approximately equal concentrations varying from 5 at% to 35 at% of five or more metal principal elements [164,165]. Nonetheless, the constitution elements have different crystal structures every so often, and a single-phase or two-phase [solid solution](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/solid-solution "Learn more about solid solution from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") crystal structure is formed. Most of the high entropy alloys tend to form normal single-phase solid solutions such as Face-Centered Cubic (FCC), Body-Centered Cubic (BCC), or a dual-phase (FCC+BCC), instead of complex compounds (like [intermetallic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/intermetallics "Learn more about intermetallic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") compounds) because of the high configuration entropy of the system except for a few Hexagonal Closed-Packed (HCP) single-phase high entropy alloys [164–167]. In recent years, significant research has been done on high entropy alloys due to the ability of these alloys to form solid solution structures with uncommon properties [168]. Among these properties, we can mention high hardness, good [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), wear resistance, [corrosion resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/corrosion-resistance "Learn more about corrosion resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), high [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), exceptional ductility, thermal stability in high and low temperatures, and enormous solid solution microstructure. As a consequence of these excellent properties, is expected to be used in a wide range of industrial applications such as aerospace, automotive, and energy [168–170].

Manufacturing complicated HEA structures with conventional technology is a challenge. [AM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about AM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a near-net-shape manufacturing technology that can overcome the limitations of conventional processing methods. [Additive manufacturing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about Additive manufacturing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can fabricate any piece with complicated geometry through layer-by-layer melting of powder [feedstock](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/feedstock "Learn more about feedstock from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") using a laser or electron beam energy. The most outstanding [additive manufacturing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about additive manufacturing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques for HEAs consist of [selective laser melting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/selective-laser-melting "Learn more about selective laser melting from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), laser melting deposition, selective electron beam melting (SEBM), powder plasma arc (PPA), and also laser-engineered net shaping [170–172].

#### 6.1 Effects of diverse strengthening mechanisms on the thermal stability of AM high entropy alloys

Fig. 37**a**, shows the variation of compressive yield strength of CoCrFeMnNi and AlCoCrFeNi 2.1 vs. temperatures; the alloys have been fabricated through [SLM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/selective-laser-melting "Learn more about SLM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and LENS methods, respectively. The thermal stability of the AM alloys at high temperatures (up to 700 °C) is noticeable. The CoCrFeMnNi high entropy alloy (SLM-AM) achieved superior mechanical properties at low and medium temperatures (25–600 °C) where the presence of fine grains, high dislocation density, and fine precipitation was denoted as the main influencing factors. At high temperatures (700 °C or higher) the high dislocation density and fine precipitates are only traced. Fig. 37 b displays EBSD-IPF maps of CoCrFeMnNi made by SLM-AM. A great number of deformation twins were seen at room temperature, 500 °C, and 600 °C (indicated by white arrows). Where yield strength drop occurred (at 700 °C or higher), at the grain boundary were observed grains of hundreds of nm up to a few μm. Commonly, such fine grains are known to be formed by DRX. Therefore, the notable decrease in flow stress above 700 °C is caused by DRX. According to the above findings, it was acknowledged that the dominant [deformation mechanisms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/deformation-mechanism "Learn more about deformation mechanisms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") were slip and deformation twinning, which happened from room temperature up to 600 °C, and slip and partial recrystallization, which happened at temperatures higher than 700 °C [173]. In the case of AlCoCrFeNi 2.1, the yield strength increases by increasing temperature from room temperature up to 400 °C, which is due to the precipitation strengthening caused by L1 2 phases precipitated in the FCC matrix and σ phases precipitated in the BCC matrix [174]. The gradually decreasing strength till 800 °C is attributed to the growth of precipitated phases at high temperatures, Fig. 37 a [173,175]. To better understand look at Fig. 37 c, which shows [EBSD](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electron-backscatter-diffraction "Learn more about EBSD from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") maps. As the EBSD maps exhibited that yield strength anomaly could be ascribed to the presence of the ordered intermetallic L1 2 phase. Since L1 2 is harder than the BCC phase, the primary [deformation mechanism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/deformation-mechanism "Learn more about deformation mechanism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") relies wholly on the L1 2 phase at higher temperatures. This boosts strength at higher temperatures [175].

##### 6.1.1 Effect of solid solution strengthening

The room temperature strengthening mechanisms of HEAs AM-built have been discussed in detail in previous studies. Here we discuss how the microstructural features of these alloys caused improved strength at high temperatures. The major strengthening mechanism in HEAs that have been fabricated by AM methods is interstitial and substitutional solute strengthening, which can clarify the difference in mechanical properties, even in high temperatures [179,180]. Exceptional solid solution strengthening is discussed relying on the lattice distortion effect, which is completely different from those observed in conventional alloys. The various neighbors of a specified alloying element in HEAs result in asymmetric bonding nature in various directions and a desire for the formation of different crystal structures; this means a high degree of lattice distortion [181].

Zhang et al. [182] have investigated CoCrFeNiX 0.4 (X = Al, Nb, Ta) HEAs fabricated by powder plasma arc additive manufacturing where X was dissolved in the FCC matrix phase. As reported in the case of CoCrFeNiTa 0.4 and CoCrFeNiNb 0.4, the formation of lave solid solution phases rich in Co-Ta and Fe-Nb, respectively, decreases the ductility values considerably. Michael Melia et al. [180] studied MoNbTaW refractory HEAs produced by one of the high-throughput processing techniques, the direct energy deposition additive manufacturing process. It has been reported that nanoscale Nb-C precipitates substantially contribute to the strengthening. As the concentration of Nb increases, the hardness increases, which indicates the prominent effect of the solid solution strengthening. [Solid solution](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/solid-solution "Learn more about Solid solution from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") strengthening has been also reported as the primary strengthening mechanism in MoNbTaWTi [183] refractory HEAs prepared through in-situ [wire arc additive manufacturing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/wire-arc-additive-manufacturing "Learn more about wire arc additive manufacturing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which represents high-temperature structural stability.

##### 6.1.2 Influence of precipitation strengthening

Precipitation strengthening is another dominant mechanism for strengthening especially in multiphase HEAs [179,182,184,185]. Nanosized precipitation in the HEAs fabricated by AM could create an ideal microstructure to achieve excellent mechanical properties [17]. Qingkai Shen et al. [184] have investigated the influence of heat treatment at 600 °C and 800 °C for 8 h on the microstructure and mechanical properties of Al-Co-Cr-Fe-Ni dual-phase (FCC+ordered BCC (B2)) high entropy alloy produced by combined cable wire arc additive manufacturing (CCW-AAM). The as-deposited alloy was composed of the FeCr-rich FCC phase and also the AlNi-rich ordered BCC (B2) phase. During heat treatment for this alloy at 600 °C, a great number of nanosized ordered FCC (L1 2) phases precipitated in the FCC matrix and the Cr-rich σ phase precipitated in the B2 matrix leading to precipitation strengthening, as a result of which, the yield and ultimate tensile strength compared with the as-deposited specimen increased. Following the same trend, the hardness values also increased. However, owing to the brittle nature of the σ phase, the ductility values decreased. By conducting the heat treatment at 800 °C, the size of the σ phase in the B2 matrix was increased causing a reduction in the effect of precipitation strengthening. The L1 2 phase was also converted into the rod-like AlNi-rich B2 phase precipitated in the FCC matrix. Consequently, the strength was decreased and was comparable to that of the as-deposited specimen. The microstructure evaluation caused by various heat treatments can be seen in the schematic diagram in Fig. 39.

##### 6.1.3 Effect of grain boundary strengthening

Another strengthening mechanism in HEAs AM-built is grain boundary strengthening [23]. The grain boundary strengthening mechanism is also used by the Hall-Petch equation to clarify changes in mechanical properties, the finer the grain size, the higher the strength of these alloys [182,183]. By reducing the grain size due to the addition of alloying elements as a grain refiner, the formation of new phases at the grain boundary is encouraged and the mobility of dislocation is decreased, as a result, the strength increases [186]. Hang Zhang et al. [187] have investigated the effect of Ti and Ni additions on the mechanical properties of NbMoTa HEA prepared by SLM-AM. By adding Ti and Ni into the NbMoTa HEA, the new phases such as Ni3Ta (Ti, Nb, Mo) main phase, (TiNi)3 Mo (Nb, Ta)2 phase, and β-Ti (Nb, Mo, Ta) phase were formed at the grain boundaries. The effect of adding Ti and Ni to the basis NbMoTa alloy has been shown in Fig. 40. As can be seen in Fig. 40 due to the addition of these alloying elements, the grain size has been greatly reduced. The addition of Ni exposes an evident refinement phenomenon and the average grain size is reduced. Moreover, the grain size distribution is relatively concentrated. Also, adding Ti and Ni simultaneously (Fig. 40 d, e) demonstrates the grain refinement effect, which can be attributed to the forming of a vast number of secondary phases caused by Ni addition. Based on the grain refinement effect, the hardness of NbMoTaNi and NbMoTaTi0.5Ni0.5 alloys is greatly improved. Therefore, the [compressive strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/compressive-strength "Learn more about compressive strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the yield strength of the NbMoTaTi 0.5 Ni 0.5 HEA increased from 1282 MPa to 2277 MPa and from 1252 MPa to1750MPa at room temperature by grain boundary strengthening, respectively. As is realized, the compressive strength has been increased through boundary strengthening even up to two order magnitude.

##### 6.1.4 Effect of dislocation strengthening mechanism

The role of dislocation networks in strengthening should be also considered [188], the concept which may be crucial in determining the strength of HEAs AM-built samples [189]. In this respect, Danyang Lin et al. [190] have emphasized the role of dislocation networks including the high frequency of geometrically necessary dislocations (GND), the presence of which is necessary for maintaining material continuity and harmonizing lattice interfaces.

The origin of GND in additively manufactured materials, especially those which have been fabricated through [powder bed fusion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/powder-bed-fusion "Learn more about powder bed fusion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") processes, can be discussed considering the high cooling rate of the solidification process and the resultant internal stresses. Dislocation networks do not consist of mobile dislocations. It is arduous for subsequent moving dislocations to cross through the performed network. Therefore, dislocations obviously aggregate inside the dislocation network. This pile-up can prevent the crossing of subsequent dislocations and accordingly results in the strengthening of the material. Table 7 shows a summary corresponding to the behavior of AM high entropy alloys and the most effective strengthening mechanisms on thermal stability at low and high temperatures.

| CrMnFeCoNi | Laser-AM | Dislocation strengthening | 20 °C to ˗196 °C | By decreasing the temperature from 20 °C to ˗196 °C, cryogenic conditions, the strength was increased |

Based on the grain refinement effect, the hardness is greatly improved, and by boundary strengthening, the compressive strength increased |

As a summarization of the effects of diverse strengthening mechanisms on the thermal stability of high entropy alloys fabricated by additive manufacturing methods as shown in Table 7, can mention interstitial and substitutional solute strengthening, the major strengthening mechanism in these alloys. Solid solution strengthening mechanism enhances strength by changing microstructural features at high temperatures. In this regard, the precipitation strengthening mechanism by precipitate nanosized in HEAs achieves excellent mechanical properties at high temperatures. The grain boundary mechanism is another mechanism in these alloys that is used by the Hall-Petch equation. Caused by adding alloying elements as a grain refiner, the grain size decreases so the strength increases owing to the formation of new phases at the grain boundary and decreasing the mobility of dislocation.

### 1 Introduction

High-entropy alloys are generally defined as alloys composed of five or more main elements [1,2]. According to the equal atomic ratio or near equal atomic ratio, the content of each component in the alloy is between 5% and 35% [3]. Because of their high mixing entropy, the Gibbs free energy is low, so high-entropy alloys tend to form stable, [solid solutions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/solid-solution "Learn more about solid solutions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [4–7]. It has been found that HEAs have good [corrosion resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/corrosion-resistance "Learn more about corrosion resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [8,9], thermal stability [10], unique electrical and [magnetic properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/magnetic-property "Learn more about magnetic properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [11], high hardness and [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [12,13] and excellent wear resistance [14]. These characteristics make them widely used in many fields, such as new energy materials, aerospace materials, and environmentally friendly materials [15,16].

Traditional metal materials usually improve their properties by heat treatment, which is considered as an economical, rapid and effective means of modification [17,18]. Heat treatment technology in high-entropy alloys has also been widely used [19–21]. The microstructure of the AlCoCrFeNiTi 0.8 high-entropy alloy had no obvious change after heat treatment at 700°C. After heat treatment at 1200°C, the coarsening behavior of the AlNi precipitate was observed, and finally, the wear resistance of the coating was reduced [22]. The Al 0.5 CoCrFeMnNi high entropy alloy showed good plasticity and toughness after cold rolling and annealing at different temperatures [23]. The hardness of the HfNbTaTiZr high-entropy alloy increased by 35% to 500 HV after aging at 600°C for 10 h [24].

# What Are High Entropy Alloys?

![Shaheer Rehan](https://www.azonetwork.com/themes/clients/images/team/shaheer.jpg)

**High entropy alloys (HEAs) are a novel class of metallic materials that have attracted significant attention in the materials science community in recent years due to their unique combination of mechanical, thermal, and physical properties.**

![What Are High Entropy Alloys?](https://www.azonano.com/images/Article_Images/ImageForArticle_6374_16756906371564119.jpg)

![What Are High Entropy Alloys?](https://www.azonano.com/images/Article_Images/ImageForArticle_6374_16756906371564119.jpg)

*Image Credit: KickaPoo/Shutterstock.com*

## An Introduction to High Entropy Alloys

The term “high entropy” refers to the fact that these alloys contain five or more elements in roughly equal amounts, resulting in a complex microstructure and high configurational entropy.

This high configurational entropy results from the presence of many different types of atoms in the alloys, which leads to complex and heterogeneous structures that can be manipulated to achieve desirable properties.

High entropy alloys are typically composed of elements with different crystal structures and exhibit several unique properties compared to conventional alloys. HEAs often possess high strength, hardness, wear resistance, thermal stability, and corrosion resistance.

They can exhibit improved thermal and electrical conductivity and magnetic properties, making them suitable for a wide range of applications in various fields, such as [aerospace](/article.aspx?ArticleID=3103), energy, transportation, and biomedical engineering.

HEAs are typically produced through various synthesis methods, such as casting, powder metallurgy, and mechanical alloying.

The properties of HEAs can be tailored through variations in composition, microstructure, and processing conditions. For instance, the microstructure of HEAs can be controlled by adjusting the processing conditions, including temperature, cooling rate, and heat treatment.

This enables engineers to produce HEAs with specific mechanical, thermal, electrical, and magnetic properties that are optimized for specific applications.

## Applications of High Entropy Alloys

High entropy alloys have been identified as having potential for a wide range of applications due to their unique combination of mechanical, thermal, and physical properties.

### Related Stories

As indicated by [Dada](https://doi.org/10.5772/intechopen.84982), HEAs may be used for high-temperature applications, such as in jet engines and hypersonic vehicles, due to their high-temperature strength and thermal stability.

High entropy alloys have also been considered for use in [automotive](/article.aspx?ArticleID=5906) applications due to their high strength and toughness, making them suitable for structural components, such as engine parts, suspension systems, and drivetrain components.

Another area in which HEAs can play an important role is orthopedic implants. [de Oliveira](https://doi.org/10.3390/met12111940) showed that HEAs have excellent corrosion resistance and biocompatibility, making them suitable for use as biomaterials for medical applications.

## Challenges Faced by High Entropy Alloys

Although HEAs have garnered significant attention for their unique combination of properties, there are still a number of challenges associated with their synthesis, processing, and characterization.

One of the main challenges is the development of cost-effective and scalable synthesis techniques for these complex alloys.

Furthermore, the lack of a clear understanding of the relationship between composition, microstructure, and properties makes it difficult to accurately predict the properties of these alloys, which can limit their widespread adoption in industry.

## New Research in the Field of High-Entropy Alloys

Due to their potential applications, HEAs have been the subject of intense research and development in recent years. Researchers have been working to understand the properties of HEAs and to develop new alloys with improved properties for specific applications.

One of the key areas of research in the field of HEAs is the study of structure-property relationships. Researchers are working to understand how the microstructure of HEAs affects their properties and how these properties can be tailored for specific applications.

A recent study has shown that adding small amounts of cerium to a high-entropy alloy with a high concentration of nickel and cobalt improves the high-temperature strength of the alloy.

An important research topic in the field of HEAs is the study of mechanical properties. Researchers are working to understand how the mechanical properties of HEAs are influenced by their composition, microstructure, and processing conditions.

[Zhang](https://doi.org/10.3390/met13010178) analyzed the effects of different elements on the microstructure and mechanical properties of high entropy alloys containing nickel, cobalt, and aluminum.

Another research paper reported that the high-temperature strength of a high-entropy alloy with a high concentration of nickel, cobalt, and aluminum can be improved by changing the processing conditions used to produce the alloy.

High entropy alloys are also being studied for their corrosion resistance, and researchers are working to understand how the corrosion resistance of HEAs is influenced by their composition and microstructure.

Recently published research has shown that adding small amounts of chromium to a high-entropy alloy with a high concentration of nickel and cobalt improves the corrosion resistance of the alloy.

HEAs are also being studied for their biocompatibility, and researchers are working to understand how the biocompatibility of HEAs is influenced by their composition and microstructure.

de Oliveira has documented that HEAs with high levels of titanium and molybdenum have good biocompatibility and can be used as biomaterials for medical applications.

## To Conclude

HEAs are a new and rapidly growing area of research in the field of materials science. Despite some challenges, the potential applications of HEAs are vast, and ongoing research is focused on understanding their properties and optimizing them for specific applications.

With continued research and development, the use of HEAs is expected to expand in the coming years and significantly impact a range of industries.

5 Applications 5.1 Application of cutting tools.
Comparing the friction and wear behavior of high entropy coating tool and ordinary high speed steel[16]. When the external load and rotational speed are fixed, the friction of the high entropy alloy coated cutting tool is always lower than that of the ordinary high speed steel. When the tool is rotating at high speed, the higher the friction coefficient is, the higher the friction resistance will be. At this time the chip is very easy to stick knife, and it will scratch the surface of the material, which will reduce the surface finish of the material.
Accordingly, the high entropy alloy coating on the surface of the cutting tool can reduce the friction force in the cutting process, which will prolong the tool life and improve the surface quality of the machined surface. 5.2 Application of hydrogen storage.
Many researches and experiments show that some high entropy alloys have excellent hydrogen storage function. Qiu Jianping studied the hydrogen storage properties of these three kinds of high entropy alloys, which are respectively ScTiVCrCo, ScTiVCrNi, ScTiVCrCu. The results show that the maximum hydrogen absorption capacity of the three alloys is respectively 1.76%, 2.19%, and 2. 56%. With the increase of the number of 4 MATEC Web of Conferences 142, 03003 (2018) https://doi.org/10.1051/matecconf/201814203003 ICMAE2017 hydrogen absorption, the amount of hydrogen absorption will be reduced correspondingly [17].
5.3 Application of chemical corrosion.
Because of eminent corrosion resistance, the high entropy alloy can be applied to all kinds of corrosive working circumstances such as the bottom of ocean ship, the occasions which are prone to electrochemical reaction and various chemical vessels, etc..
5.4 High load and high impact applications.
High entropy alloys can be used to produce high quality golf hitting surfaces, high hardness steel pipe and turbine blade, which work in high load and high circumstances. High entropy alloys can also be used for national defense science and technology, such as the track of tanks, artillery shells, etc.

enriched_points.expanded_content: High-Entropy Alloys (HEAs) consist of at least five major metallic elements, enhancing their mechanical properties through diverse atomic interactions and reducing brittleness compared to traditional alloys.
HEAs exhibit near-equiatomic compositions, where each element contributes approximately equally, resulting in high configurational entropy and promoting the stabilization of solid solution phases.
The considerable configurational entropy in HEAs arises from multiple elements, leading to unique microstructures that enhance thermal stability, corrosion resistance, and mechanical strength.
HEAs are known for their exceptional properties, including high strength, hardness, wear resistance, and corrosion resistance, making them suitable for demanding applications in various industries.
Due to their desirable properties, HEAs are utilized in aerospace, automotive, energy, and biomedical sectors, showing potential for high-temperature and high-stress applications.
